title: Impact of Weekend Admission on In-Hospital Outcomes in Patients with Acute Myocardial Infarction Undergoing Percutaneous Coronary Intervention
text:
Reference Studies:

Liu et al., 2024

Study Objective: To evaluate the association between weekend admission and in-hospital outcomes among adult patients hospitalized for acute myocardial infarction who underwent percutaneous coronary intervention.
Data Source: A retrospective cross-sectional study using the National Inpatient Sample (NIS), years 2018вҖ“2020.
Cohort Definition: Hospitalizations were included if they met the following criteria

Age вүҘ 18 years
Principal diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction
Underwent percutaneous coronary intervention
Non-missing values for admission timing

Exposure of Interest: Weekend admission, categorizing admissions as SaturdayвҖ“Sunday (weekend) vs.В MondayвҖ“Friday (weekday).
Outcomes of Interest:

Primary Outcome:

In-hospital mortality

Secondary Outcomes:

Length of stay (LOS), in days
Inflation-adjusted total hospital charges (in 2020 USD)
Mechanical ventilation use
Favorable discharge (discharge to home or home health; all other dispositions were classified as unfavorable)
Cardiogenic Shock
Cardaic arrest
Acute kidney injury
GI hemorrhage
Stroke
Pulmonary artery catheterization
Sepsis
Urinary tract infection
Undergoing CABG
Mechanical circulatory support
Coronary artery dissection
Coronary artery perforation
Postprocedural complications
Ventricular arrhythmia
Blood transfusion
Recurrent MI during the hospital stay
Intra-aortic balloon pump
Hemodialysis


Covariates for Adjustment: All models adjusted for the following

Demographic Factors: Age, sex, race/ethnicity
Socioeconomic Factors: Primary payer, ZIP codeвҖ“based income quartile
Clinical Factors: Elixhauser comorbidity index, thrombolytic drugs given, type of acute myocardial infarction (STEMI or NSTEMI)
Hospital-Level Factors: Hospital region, bed size, and teaching status

Statistical Methods:

Univariable Analysis:

Categorical variables: Compared using PearsonвҖҷs chi-square test with Rao & Scott adjustment
Continuous variables: Analyzed using the design-based KruskalвҖ“Wallis test, a non-parametric alternative suitable for survey-weighted data

Multivariable Analysis:

Binary outcomes (e.g., mortality, mechanical ventilation, discharge): Survey-weighted logistic regression using the quasibinomial family
Continuous outcomes (e.g., LOS, hospital charges): Survey-weighted linear regression using the gaussian family
Results reported as adjusted odds ratios (aORs) or beta coefficients with 95% confidence intervals


Software: All statistical analyses were performed using the R Statistical Language (Version 4.5.0; R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria), incorporating survey-weighted procedures via the survey package to account for the complex sampling design of NIS.
